behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Unit, because this is retry-delay math/state progression and cap enforcement for consecutive failures. Downgrade check: cannot go lower than Unit. Upgrade check: no behavior text requires module handoff, real network/OS/subprocess, or model output. Rejected higher level because the behavior only states delay growth and max cap logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure delay-calculation/retry-state function with consecutive failure counts (1..N) and config max delay; assert exponential sequence and hard cap at configured maximum.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, because failure can occur at the boundary between response classification and retry scheduler (status/error payload -> retry decision + delay). Downgrade check: a single-function test could miss wiring mismatches between classifier and retry logic. Upgrade check: behavior does not require real HTTP transport; an in-process stub response is sufficient. Rejected System because text says only 'identifies a rate-limit response and computes wait time' without requiring real network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator + response classifier with a stub transport returning a 429/rate-limit-shaped response; assert it is recognized as retryable and that computed delay matches policy/header parsing rules.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, since this needs retry orchestrator, timer/scheduler, request replay, and result propagation working together. Downgrade check: Unit on one function would not prove resend of the original request through the call() boundary. Upgrade check: no explicit requirement for real OS/network/subprocess; fake clock + stub transport can reproduce failure. Rejected System because behavior is about orchestration, not real infrastructure effects.
behaviors.test_description: Using fake timers and a stub API client, force first attempt to return rate-limit then second to succeed; assert the same request payload is resent after backoff and call() resolves with the successful response without exposing retry internals.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed QuotaExhaustedError rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, because max-retry counting, termination, and public error mapping must align across modules. Downgrade check: pure Unit may miss mismatch where internal retry exhaustion is not mapped to exported typed error at call() boundary. Upgrade check: no real infra or AI dependency in behavior text. Rejected System because behavior only requires correct typed error emission after configured retries.
behaviors.test_description: Configure low max retries, stub transport to always return rate-limit, execute call(), and assert rejection is specifically QuotaExhaustedError (type/instance and key fields), not a generic Error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, because this explicitly spans multiple local components (retry chain + quota tracking) and termination conditions. Downgrade check: Unit cannot validate full chain termination across interacting modules. Upgrade check: behavior does not require real OS/network; controlled stubs and fake timers can detect infinite-loop risk. Rejected System because no concrete text requires real HTTP/subprocess timing behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run call() with transport continuously rate-limiting and fake timers advanced deterministically; assert attempts stop at expected bound and promise settles with QuotaExhaustedError within finite simulated time.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, as this is shared-state correctness across concurrent call() invocations and quota manager wiring. Downgrade check: Unit of a quota function misses real async contention through public API. Upgrade check: behavior is explicitly 'same process' and does not require real network/OS primitives. Rejected System because the failure boundary is in-process concurrency and shared module state, not external infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota manager, fire multiple call() promises concurrently with controlled stub responses, and assert aggregate consumption/denials reflect one shared bucket across callers rather than per-caller buckets.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration, because queueing discipline (FIFO) must hold through async scheduler + dispatcher interaction. Downgrade check: Unit queue test alone may not catch reordering introduced by dispatcher/timer wiring. Upgrade check: no behavior text requires real OS scheduler or real network; deterministic in-process harness can expose ordering bugs. Rejected System because ordering requirement is internal dispatch order, not external infrastructure behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several call() requests while sending is blocked/rate-limited, then release capacity stepwise via stubs; record dispatch sequence and assert it matches original submission order exactly.
